refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve a measured p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target for the load→travel→dump cycle under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, with cycle time performance monitored and reported via telemetry.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-002 is a key performance indicator (KPI) requirement that directly impacts customer productivity and system acceptance. Refinement adds explicit reference to measurement/monitoring (telemetry) and duty cycle context to make verification concrete. High safety criticality tailoring requires that cycle time optimization does not compromise safety interlocks or emergency response.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrumented field trials under representative duty cycle; p95 cycle time computed from telemetry logs; comparison against CT_target acceptance threshold; safety event correlation analysis to ensure no safety compromise.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The vehicle shall execute load, travel, and dump sub-cycles with deterministic timing within design envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall collect and transmit cycle time telemetry (start, end, sub-phase timestamps) to enable p95 KPI calculation.
FR-NGHE-002.3: Safety services shall not introduce blocking delays that degrade cycle time below CT_target.
FR-NGHE-002.4: Teleoperation latency and control responsiveness shall not prevent achievement of CT_target under normal network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Optimize vehicle hydraulic/mechanical actuation timing and parallel sub-cycle execution (load while positioning for travel); minimize idle states.
DP-B: Implement predictive cycle scheduling with operator coaching and real-time cycle time feedback via telemetry dashboard.
DP-C: Integrate safety services as non-blocking asynchronous monitors; use safety-critical fast-path for emergency stop, defer non-critical checks to background.
DP-D: Provision network bandwidth and latency SLA for teleoperation to ensure control loop closure time ≤ acceptable threshold.
selected_dp.selected_dp: Composite DP: Combine DP-A (vehicle mechanical optimization), DP-C (non-blocking safety architecture), and DP-D (network SLA provisioning). DP-B (operator coaching) is secondary and deferred to next decomposition level.

potential_sources: Vehicle cycle timing (DP-A) and safety service latency (DP-C) are coupled: safety checks must not serialize with load/travel/dump phases. Mitigation: asynchronous safety monitoring with fast-path emergency stop.

Teleoperation control latency (DP-D) and vehicle actuation timing (DP-A) are coupled: network delay must not exceed operator reaction time budget. Mitigation: SLA specification and fallback to autonomous mode.
Telemetry collection (sub-FR 002.2) and cycle execution (DP-A) are coupled: instrumentation overhead must be negligible. Mitigation: lightweight async telemetry logging.
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable via architectural separation (asynchronous safety, network SLA, lightweight telemetry). No fundamental design contradiction identified.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle hydraulic/mechanical subsystem can achieve target cycle time without exceeding power/thermal budgets. Risk: If not, DP-A may require redesign of actuation system (high cost/schedule impact).
Assumption: Network infrastructure at customer site meets SLA (latency ≤ X ms, availability ≥ Y%). Risk: If not, teleoperation may not be viable; fallback to autonomous mode required.

Assumption: Safety services can be architected as non-blocking monitors without compromising safety integrity. Risk: If safety logic is inherently sequential, DP-C may not be feasible; requires safety case review.
Assumption: Operator coaching and feedback (DP-B) can improve cycle time by Y% without additional hardware. Risk: Behavioral change may not materialize; DP-B may be ineffective.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Proceed with composite DP (A+C+D). Defer DP-B to next level. Establish explicit SLA for network latency and safety service response time as design constraints. Conduct safety case review to validate non-blocking safety architecture.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Top-level system integration boundary encompassing vehicle, teleoperation, and safety services. Responsible for end-to-end cycle time performance, telemetry aggregation, and coordination of sub-system timing.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Cycle time telemetry (load start, travel start, dump start, dump end timestamps) transmitted to external monitoring/analytics system for p95 KPI calculation and operator feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator or autonomous planner issues load/travel/dump task commands; NGHE Solution executes and reports completion.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety services emit alerts (e.g., emergency stop, geofence breach, stability warning) that may interrupt or modify cycle execution.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional network connectivity for teleoperation control commands (in) and telemetry/status (out); subject to latency SLA.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level integration boundary for cycle time KPI. All sub-systems (vehicle, teleoperation, safety) are logically contained. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) cross boundary.

Vehicle module owns mechanical/hydraulic actuation timing (DP-A core). However, cycle time KPI requires coordination with teleoperation (control latency) and safety services (non-blocking architecture). Assigning to Vehicle alone would diffuse responsibility for network SLA and safety integration.

Teleoperation owns network latency and control responsiveness (DP-D). However, cycle time KPI also depends on vehicle actuation timing and safety service latency. Assigning to Teleoperation alone would diffuse responsibility for mechanical optimization and safety integration.

Safety Services owns non-blocking safety architecture (DP-C). However, cycle time KPI is not primarily a safety concern; assigning to Safety Services would diffuse responsibility for vehicle and teleoperation performance.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary because: (1) Cycle time KPI is a system-level performance metric that integrates vehicle, teleoperation, and safety sub-systems. (2) No single sub-module (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) can independently achieve the KPI; coordination across all three is required. (3) NGHE Solution provides the integration layer to orchestrate timing, monitor telemetry, and enforce SLAs. (4) Diffusion is minimal: external interfaces are well-defined (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link).
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution is a high-level integration boundary; detailed cycle time optimization logic may be diffused across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules. Mitigation: Establish explicit timing contracts (SLAs) for each sub-module and enforce via integration testing.

Risk: Telemetry collection and p95 calculation may be assigned to a separate analytics system outside NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Clarify whether telemetry aggregation is part of NGHE Solution or external; if external, define Telemetry interface contract clearly.
Risk: Safety Services may introduce unexpected latency if not properly architected as non-blocking. Mitigation: Conduct safety case review and performance testing to validate DP-C feasibility.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assign FR-NGHE-002 to NGHE Solution as the integration boundary. Establish explicit sub-module SLAs: Vehicle (cycle sub-phase timing), Teleoperation (network latency ≤ X ms), Safety Services (non-blocking response time ≤ Y ms). Define Telemetry interface contract (timestamp precision, transmission frequency). Defer detailed allocation to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services sub-modules to next decomposition level.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected composite DP (A+C+D) directly addresses refined FR: DP-A optimizes vehicle cycle timing, DP-C ensures safety does not block cycle execution, DP-D provisions network SLA for teleoperation. Couplings are identified and mitigations proposed. Assumptions and risks are documented. DP is feasible and testable.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: Vehicle mechanical optimization (DP-A) is owned by Vehicle module, but cycle time KPI is owned by NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Establish explicit timing SLA for Vehicle sub-module; NGHE Solution monitors and enforces.

Diffusion Risk 2: Safety service non-blocking architecture (DP-C) is owned by Safety Services module, but cycle time KPI is owned by NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Establish explicit latency SLA for Safety Services; NGHE Solution monitors and enforces.

Diffusion Risk 3: Network SLA (DP-D) is owned by Teleoperation module, but cycle time KPI is owned by NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Establish explicit network latency SLA for Teleoperation; NGHE Solution monitors and enforces.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for cycle time KPI because it integrates all three sub-systems. Diffusion is managed via explicit SLA contracts between NGHE Solution and sub-modules. No single sub-module can be assigned the full KPI responsibility.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via instrumented field trials: (1) Collect cycle time telemetry (timestamps for load, travel, dump phases). (2) Compute p95 cycle time from telemetry logs. (3) Compare against CT_target. (4) Correlate with safety events to ensure no safety compromise. (5) Validate network latency and vehicle timing via sub-module testing.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns cycle time KPI; Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services own sub-system SLAs. Telemetry interface is defined. Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces are defined. Next level decomposition will allocate sub-FRs to sub-modules.

recommended_revisions: Establish explicit SLA targets for Vehicle (cycle sub-phase timing), Teleoperation (network latency), and Safety Services (non-blocking response time). Document in design specification.
Conduct safety case review to validate non-blocking safety architecture (DP-C) does not compromise safety integrity.
Define Telemetry interface contract: timestamp precision (e.g., ±10 ms), transmission frequency (e.g., per cycle), data format (JSON, protobuf, etc.).
Establish network SLA verification plan: measure latency under representative site conditions; validate fallback to autonomous mode if SLA is violated.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-002 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (002.1, 002.2, 002.3, 002.4) that address vehicle timing, telemetry, safety integration, and teleoperation latency respectively. Each sub-FR requires further decomposition to assign to specific sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and define detailed design parameters. NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary at this level, but next level must allocate sub-FRs to sub-modules and establish SLA contracts.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The vehicle shall execute load, travel, and dump sub-cycles with deterministic timing within design envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall collect and transmit cycle time telemetry (start, end, sub-phase timestamps) to enable p95 KPI calculation.
FR-NGHE-002.3: Safety services shall not introduce blocking delays that degrade cycle time below CT_target.
FR-NGHE-002.4: Teleoperation latency and control responsiveness shall not prevent achievement of CT_target under normal network conditions.

assumptions: Vehicle hydraulic/mechanical subsystem can achieve target cycle time without exceeding power/thermal budgets.
Network infrastructure at customer site meets SLA (latency ≤ X ms, availability ≥ Y%).
Safety services can be architected as non-blocking monitors without compromising safety integrity.
Operator coaching and feedback can improve cycle time by Y% without additional hardware.
Telemetry collection overhead is negligible (< 1% of cycle time).
p95 cycle time is a valid KPI for customer acceptance (not p50 or p99).
open_risks: Vehicle actuation system may not achieve target cycle time; redesign required (high cost/schedule impact).
Network infrastructure at customer site may not meet SLA; fallback to autonomous mode required.
Safety services may introduce unexpected latency if not properly architected; safety case review required.
Operator coaching may not materialize; behavioral change may be ineffective.
Telemetry collection may introduce latency; instrumentation overhead must be validated.
p95 KPI may not correlate with customer satisfaction; acceptance criteria may need revision.
evidence_needed: Vehicle cycle timing analysis: sub-phase durations, actuation timing, power/thermal budgets.
Network SLA specification: latency, availability, jitter; site survey to validate feasibility.
Safety case review: non-blocking safety architecture, response time analysis, safety integrity validation.
Telemetry design: timestamp precision, transmission frequency, data format, collection overhead analysis.
Field trial plan: instrumented load→travel→dump cycles, p95 KPI calculation, safety event correlation.
Operator feedback mechanism: cycle time dashboard, coaching logic, behavioral change validation.
